author: @alexscarbro796
text:
So I guess at lower speed, the voltage generated wasn’t high enough to fully forward bias the rectifier diodes, hence no current was really flowing.

Have you tried out an “ideal” rectifier which use MOSFETs with body diodes?

author: @azlandpilotcar4450
text: Those blade attachment bolt holes should provide a good point to stick on a flanged, small diameter pulley or gear.  If your pedals drove the small gear in the cassette, and the largest gear in the cassette drove another chain, you might get an impressive final drive ratio for  the generator.  Good project.

author: @darthfate2139
text: Next time get some laundry washmachine from scrapyard. Many modern types use 3ph bldc motor wired for 400V or so.

replies.author: @ScarletFlames1
replies.text:
They're only really good at even HIGHER speeds than this. But yeah, the faster the speed required to get to 100% operational capacity the more efficient a generator is.. generally.

What you really need for wind turbines is a CVT gear drive to convert even low rotational speeds into high RPM, but current CVT's tend to not be very durable.. CVT's also lack high performance so you'd have a hard limit of how much energy you can put through a single CVT and thus maximizing the size of a CVT geared wind turbine. They're also significantly more expensive than other type of gearing.

replies.author: @EnterTheRealm
replies.text: The magnets are ceramic and he's running directly of the 3 phase leads, good for a motor configuration but not a generator, he would have to make a connection too the common, else separate the phases and rectify each one. And the magnets are crappy ceramic ones I'm guessing.
